question: A 62-year-old man with hypertension and diabetes presents with acute substernal chest pressure for 90 minutes. BP 150/92, HR 104. ECG shows ST elevation of 3mm in V1-V4 with reciprocal ST depression in II, III, aVF. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Aspirin 324mg chewed + ticagrelor 180mg + heparin + activate cath lab for primary PCI
Aspirin + nitroglycerin infusion + admit for stress testing in the morning
Administer tenecteplase immediately since patient is within thrombolytic window
CT angiogram to rule out aortic dissection before antiplatelet therapy
Aspirin + clopidogrel + admit to CCU for catheterization tomorrow morning

explanation.why_correct: Classic anterior STEMI requires immediate dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor), anticoagulation (heparin), and emergent PCI. Door-to-balloon goal ≤90 minutes. Primary PCI preferred over thrombolytics when available within 120 minutes due to superior reperfusion rates (TIMI 3 flow 90-95% vs 50-60%), lower intracranial hemorrhage risk (0.1% vs 1%), and reduced re-occlusion.

why_wrong.b: STEMI with ST elevation mandates emergent reperfusion, not observation and delayed stress testing. Every 30-minute delay increases 1-year mortality 7.5%.
why_wrong.c: Thrombolytics are indicated when PCI cannot be performed within 120 minutes. PCI is available here and preferred - lower ICH, higher reperfusion rates, better outcomes.
why_wrong.d: This presentation is classic STEMI, not dissection (no tearing pain, pulse deficit, or widened mediastinum). CT would delay life-saving reperfusion.
why_wrong.e: Delaying PCI to next morning is inappropriate. Myocardial necrosis progresses with time - salvage decreases, mortality increases.

explanation.clinical_pearl: Time is myocardium. Door-to-balloon ≤90min for PCI. If PCI unavailable within 120min, door-to-needle ≤30min for thrombolytics. Every 30-minute delay increases 1-year mortality 7.5%.

question: A 28-year-old woman with no medical history presents with sharp pleuritic chest pain for 24 hours, worse lying flat, better sitting forward. URI 1 week ago. T 38.1°C, BP 110/70, HR 98. Pericardial friction rub present. ECG: diffuse concave-up ST elevation in I, II, III, aVF, V2-V6 with PR depression in II. No reciprocal changes. Troponin I 2.5 ng/mL. Diagnosis and treatment?

options.text: Acute pericarditis; ibuprofen 600mg TID + colchicine 0.5mg BID for 3 months
STEMI; aspirin, heparin, emergent catheterization
Myocarditis; prednisone 60mg daily with rapid taper
Pulmonary embolism; enoxaparin + CT pulmonary angiography
Costochondritis; reassurance and acetaminophen PRN

explanation.why_correct: Classic acute pericarditis: pleuritic positional pain + friction rub + diffuse ST elevation without reciprocal changes + PR depression + elevated troponin (myopericarditis). Viral etiology likely post-URI. NSAIDs first-line + colchicine reduces recurrence 50% (COPE/ICAP trials). Add PPI for gastric protection.

why_wrong.b: Diffuse ST elevation without reciprocal changes = pericarditis, not STEMI. STEMI has localized ST elevation with reciprocal depression. Pleuritic positional pain is characteristic of pericarditis.
why_wrong.c: While myocarditis can present similarly, the friction rub and classic ECG favor pericarditis. Steroids NOT first-line - increase recurrence. Reserve for refractory cases.
why_wrong.d: PE presents with dyspnea, hypoxia, right heart strain (S1Q3T3), not diffuse ST elevation or friction rub. CT angiography not indicated without PE suspicion.
why_wrong.e: Costochondritis has reproducible chest wall tenderness, no ECG changes, no elevated troponin, no friction rub. These findings are specific for pericarditis.

explanation.clinical_pearl: Colchicine reduces pericarditis recurrence from 32% to 10% (COPE trial). ECG stages: 1-diffuse ST elevation+PR depression, 2-pseudonormalization, 3-diffuse T inversion, 4-normalization.

question: A 72-year-old woman with hypertension, diabetes, and chronic kidney disease (baseline Cr 1.8) presents with progressive dyspnea on exertion, orthopnea, and bilateral leg edema over 3 weeks. BP 170/95, HR 92, SpO2 91%. JVD 8cm, bibasilar crackles, S3 gallop. Echo: LVEF 40%, moderate mitral regurgitation, left atrial enlargement. BNP 1800 pg/mL. Which medication class has the BEST evidence for reducing mortality in this patient?

options.text: Sacubitril-valsartan (ARNI) titrated to maximum tolerated dose, because it reduces mortality compared to enalapril in HFrEF
Furosemide 80mg IV BID to achieve aggressive diuresis and symptom relief
Digoxin 0.125mg daily to improve cardiac contractility and reduce heart failure hospitalizations
Metoprolol succinate 25mg daily started immediately to reduce heart rate and myocardial oxygen demand
Amlodipine 10mg daily to control blood pressure and reduce afterload

explanation.why_correct: This patient has HFrEF (EF 40%) with acute decompensation. Sacubitril-valsartan (ARNI) demonstrated 20% reduction in cardiovascular death or HF hospitalization compared to enalapril in the PARADIGM-HF trial. GDMT for HFrEF includes ARNI/ACEi/ARB + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i (4 pillars). ARNI should be initiated after hemodynamic stabilization.

why_wrong.b: Furosemide is essential for decongestion but does NOT reduce mortality. It provides symptom relief only. GDMT reduces mortality; diuretics do not.
why_wrong.c: Digoxin reduces HF hospitalizations but does NOT reduce mortality (DIG trial). Used for symptom control in patients already on optimal GDMT with persistent symptoms.
why_wrong.d: Beta-blockers are essential GDMT but should be initiated when euvolemic, not during acute decompensation. Starting during decompensation can worsen symptoms.
why_wrong.e: Amlodipine is a vasodilator but has no mortality benefit in HFrEF. Not part of GDMT. May be used for persistent hypertension but not as primary HF therapy.

explanation.clinical_pearl: GDMT for HFrEF (4 pillars): ARNI/ACEi/ARB + Beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i. PARADIGM-HF: ARNI 20% reduction in CV death/HF hospitalization vs enalapril. Start beta-blockers when euvolemic. SGLT2i (Dapagliflozin, Empagliflozin) reduce mortality in HFrEF regardless of diabetes status.

question: A 75-year-old man with hypertension and prior stroke presents with palpitations and fatigue for 2 weeks. Examination: irregularly irregular pulse, HR 115. ECG: absent P waves, irregular RR intervals, narrow QRS. He is hemodynamically stable. What is the MOST important factor in determining whether to start anticoagulation?

options.text: CHA₂DS₂-VASc score, because it estimates annual stroke risk and guides anticoagulation decision
Left atrial size on echocardiogram, because larger atria have higher thrombus risk
Duration of atrial fibrillation symptoms, because AF >48 hours requires TEE before cardioversion
Ventricular rate on presentation, because rapid rates indicate higher embolic risk
Serum troponin level, because elevated troponin indicates myocardial damage and higher stroke risk

explanation.why_correct: This patient has atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response. CHA₂DS₂-VASc score estimates annual stroke risk: CHF (1), HTN (1), Age ≥75 (2), DM (1), Stroke/TIA (2), Vascular disease (1), Age 65-74 (1), Sex female (1). This patient: HTN (1) + Age ≥75 (2) + Prior Stroke (2) = score 5. Score ≥2 (men) or ≥3 (women) = anticoagulation recommended. DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban) are first-line over warfarin.

why_wrong.b: LA size is associated with AF but does NOT directly guide anticoagulation. CHA₂DS₂-VASc is the evidence-based risk stratification tool.
why_wrong.c: Duration guides cardioversion timing (TEE vs 3 weeks anticoagulation), NOT the initial anticoagulation decision. All patients with CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 need anticoagulation regardless of AF duration.
why_wrong.d: Ventricular rate guides rate control therapy (beta-blockers, CCBs), not anticoagulation. Embolic risk is determined by comorbidities, not heart rate.
why_wrong.e: Troponin can be elevated in AF due to supply-demand mismatch, but does NOT independently guide anticoagulation. CHA₂DS₂-VASc remains the standard risk assessment.

explanation.clinical_pearl: CHA₂DS₂-VASc: CHF(1), HTN(1), Age≥75(2), DM(1), Stroke/TIA(2), Vascular(1), Age 65-74(1), Sex female(1). Score 0: no anticoagulation. Score 1: consider. Score ≥2: anticoagulation. DOACs preferred over warfarin (better safety, no INR monitoring). HAS-BLED for bleeding risk.

question: A 65-year-old man with hypertension and diabetes presents with acute-onset severe tearing chest pain radiating to his back. BP 180/100 right arm, 130/80 left arm. He is diaphoretic and appears acutely ill. ECG shows sinus tachycardia with nonspecific ST changes. CXR shows widened mediastinum. What is the most appropriate immediate step?

options.text: Start esmolol infusion to reduce heart rate and blood pressure, then obtain CT angiography of the chest
Administer aspirin, clopidogrel, and heparin for suspected ACS and activate the cardiac catheterization laboratory
Administer alteplase (tPA) for suspected STEMI given the severe chest pain and ECG changes
Start nitroprusside infusion alone to rapidly reduce blood pressure to normal levels
Obtain transesophageal echocardiogram (TEE) as the first diagnostic test

explanation.why_correct: This patient has classic Stanford Type A aortic dissection: acute tearing chest pain radiating to back, pulse deficit (BP differential between arms), widened mediastinum on CXR. The immediate step is blood pressure control to reduce aortic wall stress (esmolol first to reduce HR and dP/dt, then vasodilators) followed by definitive imaging (CT angiography). Beta-blockade must be started BEFORE vasodilators to prevent reflex tachycardia which increases aortic wall stress.

why_wrong.b: Antiplatelet and anticoagulation are CONTRAINDICATED in aortic dissection. They worsen bleeding and increase mortality. ACS protocol would delay correct diagnosis and potentially kill the patient if this is a dissection extending to coronary ostia.
why_wrong.c: Thrombolytics are ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in aortic dissection. Administering tPA would cause catastrophic hemorrhage and death. The ECG does NOT show STEMI (only nonspecific changes).
why_wrong.d: Vasodilators alone WITHOUT beta-blockade can cause reflex tachycardia and increased dP/dt, worsening the dissection. Beta-blockers MUST precede vasodilators. Also, BP should not be lowered too rapidly.
why_wrong.e: TEE is excellent for diagnosing dissection but should NOT delay blood pressure control. BP control should begin immediately while arranging definitive imaging. CT angiography is more readily available and less invasive.

explanation.clinical_pearl: Stanford A (ascending aorta) = surgical emergency. Stanford B (descending only) = medical management. Beta-blockers BEFORE vasodilators to avoid reflex tachycardia. Target SBP 100-120 and HR <60. CT angiography is the diagnostic gold standard. NEVER give antiplatelets or thrombolytics.

question: A 55-year-old woman with no significant medical history presents with acute dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and hemoptysis that started suddenly 6 hours after a 14-hour international flight. BP 100/65, HR 118, RR 28, SpO2 89% on room air. Examination reveals clear lungs, tachycardia, and mild right calf tenderness. ECG shows sinus tachycardia with S1Q3T3 pattern. CXR is normal. What is the most likely diagnosis and best next step?

options.text: Pulmonary embolism; start low-molecular-weight heparin and order CT pulmonary angiography
Community-acquired pneumonia; start ceftriaxone and azithromycin empirically
Acute coronary syndrome; administer aspirin, heparin, and obtain serial troponins
Pneumothorax; obtain stat chest X-ray and prepare for needle decompression
Acute asthma exacerbation; administer albuterol nebulizer and IV methylprednisolone

explanation.why_correct: This patient has classic pulmonary embolism: acute dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, risk factor (prolonged travel), tachycardia, hypoxia, clear lungs, normal CXR, S1Q3T3 on ECG, and calf tenderness suggesting DVT. Wells criteria score is high (HR >100 + hemoptysis + clinical signs of DVT + PE is #1 diagnosis). Start anticoagulation immediately with LMWH while awaiting CT pulmonary angiography for confirmation.

why_wrong.b: Pneumonia would present with productive cough, fever, and infiltrate on CXR. This patient has clear CXR and no cough. The Wells criteria strongly favor PE over pneumonia.
why_wrong.c: ACS would present with chest pressure (not pleuritic), ST changes (not S1Q3T3), and would not explain the clear lungs with hypoxia. Troponin can be elevated in PE but is not specific.
why_wrong.d: Pneumothorax causes absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, and visible pleural line on CXR. This patient has clear lungs and normal CXR. Needle decompression is for tension PTX only.
why_wrong.e: Asthma causes wheezing, prolonged expiration, and would not cause pleuritic pain or S1Q3T3 pattern. The normal lung exam and CXR rule out severe asthma.

explanation.clinical_pearl: Wells criteria for PE: clinical signs of DVT (3), PE is #1 diagnosis (3), HR >100 (1.5), immobilization/surgery <4wk (1.5), prior DVT/PE (1.5), hemoptysis (1), cancer (1). Score >4 = PE likely → CTPA. Score ≤4 = PE unlikely → D-dimer. Start anticoagulation BEFORE imaging if high suspicion.

question: A 58-year-old man with known heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF 30%) presents for routine follow-up. He is currently on lisinopril 20mg daily, carvedilol 25mg BID, and furosemide 40mg daily. BP 120/75, HR 62, eGFR 55 mL/min, K+ 4.2. He reports walking 2 blocks before becoming dyspneic (NYHA Class II). Which medication addition has the BEST evidence for reducing mortality in this patient?

options.text: Spironolactone 25mg daily, because it reduces mortality by 30% in NYHA II-IV HFrEF (RALES trial)
Digoxin 0.125mg daily, because it reduces heart failure hospitalizations and improves symptoms
Amiodarone 200mg daily, because it prevents sudden cardiac death in patients with cardiomyopathy
Ivabradine 5mg BID, because it reduces heart rate and cardiovascular mortality in HFrEF
Isosorbide dinitrate plus hydralazine, because this combination has mortality benefit in African Americans

explanation.why_correct: This patient is on ACEi + beta-blocker + diuretic but has persistent NYHA Class II symptoms. Spironolactone (MRA) added to standard therapy reduced mortality by 30% (RRR) and heart failure hospitalizations by 35% in the RALES trial in patients with NYHA III-IV HFrEF (EF ≤35%). EMPHASIS-HF extended these benefits to NYHA II patients. His K+ 4.2 and eGFR 55 are acceptable for starting MRA with monitoring.

why_wrong.b: Digoxin reduces HF hospitalizations but does NOT reduce mortality (DIG trial). It is used for symptom control in patients already on optimal GDMT with persistent symptoms. It is a third-line agent.
why_wrong.c: Amiodarone is an antiarrhythmic, not a mortality-reducing agent for HFrEF. It may be used for symptomatic arrhythmias but does NOT improve survival. Routine use is not recommended.
why_wrong.d: Ivabradine reduces cardiovascular mortality and HF hospitalizations when added to optimal GDMT in patients with HR >70 bpm despite beta-blockers (SHIFT trial). This patient's HR is 62, so ivabradine is not indicated.
why_wrong.e: ISDN/Hydralazine reduced mortality by 43% in African Americans with HFrEF (A-HeFT trial). This patient is not identified as African American, and this combination is specifically recommended for that population.

explanation.clinical_pearl: GDMT for HFrEF (4 pillars): ARNI/ACEi/ARB + Beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i. RALES: Spironolactone 25mg daily reduced mortality 30% in NYHA III-IV. EMPHASIS-HF: Eplerenone reduced mortality in NYHA II. DAPA-HF/EMPEROR-Reduced: SGLT2i reduce mortality regardless of diabetes. Monitor K+ and Cr 1 week after starting MRA.

question: A 34-year-old man presents with palpitations that started suddenly while playing basketball. He feels lightheaded but has not lost consciousness. BP 105/70, HR 180. ECG shows a regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 180 bpm with no visible P waves. Carotid sinus massage does not terminate the arrhythmia. What is the most likely diagnosis and best immediate management?

options.text: AV nodal reentrant tachycardia (AVNRT); administer adenosine 6mg rapid IV push
Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response; start diltiazem IV for rate control
Ventricular tachycardia; perform immediate synchronized cardioversion with 200J
Sinus tachycardia; administer IV fluids and treat the underlying cause
Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome; administer procainamide IV to slow accessory pathway conduction

explanation.why_correct: This is classic AVNRT: sudden onset in young healthy person, regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 180 bpm, no visible P waves (buried in QRS), and failure of carotid sinus massage. AVNRT accounts for 60% of SVT cases. Adenosine is first-line: 6mg rapid IV push → if no response → 12mg → 12mg. It transiently blocks AV node conduction, terminating the reentrant circuit. Success rate >90%.

why_wrong.b: Atrial fibrillation is IRREGULARLY irregular, not regular. This ECG shows a perfectly regular rhythm. Diltiazem is used for AF rate control, not for regular SVT.
why_wrong.c: Ventricular tachycardia produces WIDE QRS complexes (>120ms). This ECG shows narrow QRS, ruling out VT. Synchronized cardioversion is indicated only if adenosine fails or patient is unstable.
why_wrong.d: Sinus tachycardia has a gradual onset/offset, P waves before each QRS, and a rate usually <150 in young adults. HR 180 with sudden onset and no P waves is not sinus tachycardia.
why_wrong.e: WPW would show delta waves and short PR on baseline ECG. Procainamide is used for AF with WPW, not for regular SVT. Without pre-excitation on ECG, WPW is unlikely.

explanation.clinical_pearl: Adenosine for SVT: 6mg → 12mg → 12mg rapid IV push. Warn patient about transient chest discomfort, flushing, dyspnea (lasts 10-20 seconds). Success rate >90%. If unstable (hypotension, altered mental status, shock): synchronized cardioversion 50-100J. Long-term: catheter ablation if recurrent.

question: A 70-year-old man with hypertension, diabetes, and CKD stage 3 (baseline Cr 1.8) presents with syncope while shoveling snow. He recovered spontaneously within 30 seconds. His wife reports he has had progressive dyspnea on exertion and chest pressure with activity for the past 6 months. Examination reveals a harsh systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur at the right upper sternal border radiating to the carotid arteries, diminished and delayed carotid upstroke (pulsus parvus et tardus), and a sustained PMI. ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy with strain. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Severe calcific aortic stenosis causing exertional syncope, angina, and dyspnea (classic triad)
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy causing left ventricular outflow tract obstruction and syncope
Mitral valve prolapse causing mitral regurgitation and syncopal episodes
Coronary artery disease with critical left main stenosis causing exertional angina and syncope
Vasovagal syncope triggered by cold exposure and physical exertion

explanation.why_correct: This patient presents with the classic triad of severe aortic stenosis: exertional syncope (from fixed cardiac output unable to increase with exercise), angina (from increased myocardial oxygen demand with LVH), and dyspnea (from diastolic dysfunction and pulmonary congestion). Examination findings are pathognomonic: harsh systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur at RUSB, pulsus parvus et tardus (weak, delayed carotid upstroke), sustained PMI from LVH. Echo will show calcified aortic valve with severely reduced valve area (<1.0 cm²). Syncope in AS indicates critical disease with 2-3 year survival without AVR.

why_wrong.b: HCM murmur increases with Valsalva and decreases with squatting (dynamic obstruction). The murmur is usually at LLSB, not RUSB. Carotid upstroke is brisk (bifid), not delayed. Syncope in HCM is often during/after exercise, not specifically with exertion.
why_wrong.c: Mitral valve prolapse causes a mid-systolic click with late systolic murmur at apex, not RUSB. Carotid upstroke is normal. MVP does not cause the classic triad of AS. Syncope in MVP is rare and usually from arrhythmias.
why_wrong.d: CAD can cause exertional angina and LV dysfunction leading to dyspnea, but does not produce the characteristic murmur of AS or pulsus parvus et tardus. The physical exam clearly points to valvular disease.
why_wrong.e: Vasovagal syncope has prodrome (nausea, warmth, diaphoresis), occurs after prolonged standing or emotional stress, and has a slow onset. This patient's syncope was sudden during exertion without prodrome, and the exam shows clear valvular pathology.

explanation.clinical_pearl: Aortic stenosis triad: Angina, Syncope, Dyspnea (ASD). Survival without AVR: Angina 5 years, Syncope 3 years, Dyspnea 2 years. Severe AS: valve area <1.0 cm², mean gradient >40 mmHg, Vmax >4 m/s. Treatment: Surgical AVR or TAVI. Asymptomatic severe AS: Echo q6-12 months. The ONLY effective treatment is valve replacement.

question: A 45-year-old woman with end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis presents with severe weakness, palpitations, and difficulty breathing that started 6 hours after missing her dialysis session. BP 90/60, HR 45, RR 24. ECG shows sinus bradycardia, peaked T waves in all leads, widened QRS (140ms), and loss of P waves. Lab results are pending. What is the most appropriate IMMEDIATE treatment?

options.text: Calcium gluconate 1g IV over 2-3 minutes, followed by insulin 10U IV + D50, sodium bicarbonate, and emergent hemodialysis
Atropine 0.5mg IV to increase heart rate, followed by transcutaneous pacing pads placement
Normal saline 2L bolus to treat hypotension, followed by dopamine infusion for bradycardia
Amiodarone 150mg IV bolus followed by infusion for suspected ventricular arrhythmia
Immediate synchronized cardioversion at 200J for unstable bradycardia

explanation.why_correct: This patient has severe hyperkalemia from missed dialysis: peaked T waves (K+ 6-7), widened QRS and loss of P waves (K+ 7-8), bradycardia and hypotension (K+ >8). This is a life-threatening emergency. The first step is calcium gluconate IV to stabilize cardiac membranes and prevent cardiac arrest - it works within 1-3 minutes. Then shift potassium intracellularly (insulin + D50, NaHCO₃) and definitively remove potassium via emergent hemodialysis. Calcium does NOT lower potassium; it only protects the heart.

why_wrong.b: Atropine increases heart rate through vagolytic effect but does NOT address the underlying hyperkalemia. The bradycardia is from cardiac toxicity, not vagal tone. Without membrane stabilization, the patient may develop ventricular fibrillation or asystole.
why_wrong.c: IV fluids would worsen volume status in an ESRD patient and do NOT lower potassium. Dopamine for bradycardia does not treat hyperkalemia. This patient needs immediate dialysis and medical management of hyperkalemia.
why_wrong.d: Amiodarone is an antiarrhythmic that would worsen bradycardia and has no role in hyperkalemia. The ECG changes are from hyperkalemia, not a primary arrhythmia. Amiodarone could precipitate cardiac arrest.
why_wrong.e: Cardioversion treats arrhythmias, not metabolic causes. The bradycardia is from hyperkalemia toxicity, not a primary conduction disorder. Cardioversion without treating the underlying cause would be ineffective and potentially harmful.

explanation.clinical_pearl: Hyperkalemia ECG progression: Peaked T waves (K+ 5.5-6.5) → P wave flattening/disappearance (6.5-7.5) → Wide QRS (7-8) → Sine wave pattern (>8) → Cardiac arrest. Treatment order: 1-Calcium gluconate/chloride (cardioprotective), 2-Insulin+D50 (shifts K+), 3-NaHCO₃ (if acidotic), 4-Beta-agonists (albuterol), 5-Dialysis (definitive). Calcium is ALWAYS first if ECG changes present.

question: A 68-year-old woman with hypertension and atrial fibrillation (not on anticoagulation due to history of GI bleeding) presents with acute-onset severe abdominal pain out of proportion to examination findings. She is in severe distress, moaning, and diaphoretic. BP 95/60, HR 110 irregular, RR 24. Abdomen is soft with mild diffuse tenderness but no rebound or guarding. She has atrial fibrillation on monitor. Laboratory studies show WBC 18,000, lactate 6.5 mmol/L, and metabolic acidosis. CT angiography of the abdomen shows superior mesenteric artery occlusion. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Emergent surgical embolectomy with possible bowel resection, because SMA occlusion causes mesenteric ischemia requiring immediate revascularization
Start unfractionated heparin drip and admit for observation with serial abdominal examinations
Administer intra-arterial thrombolytics via catheter-directed therapy into the superior mesenteric artery
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics and admit to ICU for supportive care and blood pressure management
Perform diagnostic laparoscopy to confirm the diagnosis before proceeding with definitive treatment

explanation.why_correct: This patient has acute mesenteric ischemia from SMA embolism (AFib off anticoagulation → cardiac embolus). Pain out of proportion to exam is the classic finding. Elevated lactate and metabolic acidosis indicate bowel infarction has already begun. Emergent surgical revascularization (embolectomy) and resection of necrotic bowel is required. Without revascularization within 6-12 hours, mortality approaches 100%. The CT already confirms the diagnosis - no further diagnostic studies are needed.

why_wrong.b: Observation with heparin alone is insufficient when bowel ischemia/infarction is present. While heparin prevents further thrombus propagation, it does NOT revascularize already ischemic bowel. Surgery is mandatory.
why_wrong.c: Catheter-directed thrombolysis may be considered in selected patients without peritoneal signs, but this patient has metabolic acidosis and lactate 6.5 indicating likely bowel infarction. Thrombolytics take hours to work and the patient needs immediate revascularization.
why_wrong.d: Antibiotics are appropriate (cover gut flora) but do NOT treat the underlying vascular occlusion. Without revascularization, bowel necrosis progresses and sepsis worsens. Surgery is the definitive treatment.
why_wrong.e: CT angiography has already confirmed SMA occlusion. Diagnostic laparoscopy adds unnecessary delay. The patient needs therapeutic intervention (open surgery), not additional diagnostics.

explanation.clinical_pearl: Acute mesenteric ischemia: Pain out of proportion to exam (earliest and most sensitive finding), metabolic acidosis, elevated lactate (late). Most common cause: SMA embolism (AFib, valvular disease). Survival depends on time to revascularization: <6 hours 90% survival, >12 hours <20% survival. Treatment: Surgical embolectomy + resection of necrotic bowel. Start heparin post-operatively. Second-look laparotomy in 24-48 hours.

question: A 72-year-old man with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF 35%), hypertension, and chronic kidney disease (eGFR 35) presents with increasing dyspnea and 5kg weight gain over 2 weeks. BP 150/90, HR 78, crackles at lung bases, and 3+ bilateral pitting edema. He is on lisinopril 20mg, carvedilol 12.5mg BID, spironolactone 25mg, furosemide 80mg BID, and digoxin 0.125mg. Labs: Na 130, K+ 5.8, Cr 2.5 (baseline 1.8). ECG shows sinus rhythm, no acute changes. Which medication change is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Discontinue spironolactone, reduce or hold lisinopril, and intensify diuresis with IV furosemide while monitoring potassium closely
Add metolazone 5mg daily to potentiate furosemide and achieve more aggressive diuresis
Start sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate) 30g to treat hyperkalemia and continue all current medications
Start dopamine infusion at renal dose (2-3 mcg/kg/min) to improve renal perfusion and promote diuresis
Increase carvedilol to 25mg BID to reduce heart rate and improve cardiac output

explanation.why_correct: This patient has acute decompensated HF with significant hyperkalemia (K+ 5.8) and worsening renal function (Cr 2.5 from 1.8). The combination of ACEi + MRA + CKD + acute decompensation predisposes to life-threatening hyperkalemia. Spironolactone must be stopped immediately (major contributor to hyperkalemia). Lisinopril should be reduced or held temporarily. IV furosemide should be intensified for volume removal. Once K+ normalizes and renal function improves, these medications can be cautiously reintroduced.

why_wrong.b: Metolazone is effective for diuretic resistance but would worsen electrolyte abnormalities (hypokalemia, hyponatremia). More importantly, it does NOT address the hyperkalemia which is the most urgent issue.
why_wrong.c: Kayexalate works slowly (hours to days) and is unreliable. Continuing MRA and ACEi would continue to raise potassium. The source of hyperkalemia (spironolactone) must be removed first.
why_wrong.d: Renal-dose dopamine does NOT improve renal outcomes or mortality in acute decompensated HF (ROSE-AHF trial). It may cause tachycardia and arrhythmias. Diuresis with IV furosemide is the appropriate management.
why_wrong.e: Increasing beta-blocker during acute decompensation with hypotension (BP 150/90 but may drop) and hyperkalemia is contraindicated. Beta-blockers should be optimized when the patient is euvolemic and stable.

explanation.clinical_pearl: Hyperkalemia in HF: risk factors = ACEi/ARB + MRA + CKD + dehydration. Management: Stop MRA, hold/reduce ACEi/ARB, IV furosemide, ECG monitoring. If K+ >6.5 or ECG changes: Calcium gluconate, insulin+D50, NaHCO₃, dialysis. MRA can be restarted when K+ <5.0 and renal function improves. Never combine ACEi + ARB + MRA (increased hyperkalemia risk without mortality benefit per ONTARGET trial).

question: A 42-year-old man presents with recurrent syncope that occurs at rest, often during sleep. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. His father died suddenly at age 44. Physical examination and echocardiogram are normal. ECG shows coved ST-segment elevation of 3 mm in V1 and V2 with T-wave inversion. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) placement, because Brugada syndrome carries a high risk of sudden cardiac death
Electrophysiology study with programmed ventricular stimulation to risk-stratify before deciding on ICD
Quinidine 600mg daily as medical therapy, with ICD placement only if breakthrough arrhythmias occur
Reassurance and lifestyle modifications, because asymptomatic Brugada pattern has a benign prognosis
Catheter ablation of the right ventricular outflow tract to eliminate the arrhythmogenic substrate

explanation.why_correct: This patient has Brugada syndrome Type 1: spontaneous coved ST elevation in V1-V2 with T-wave inversion, syncope (likely due to polymorphic VT/VF), and family history of sudden cardiac death. He is HIGH RISK: spontaneous Type 1 pattern + syncope + family history. ICD is the ONLY proven therapy to prevent sudden death in high-risk Brugada patients. This is a Class I indication per guidelines.

why_wrong.b: This patient is already high-risk (spontaneous pattern + syncope + family history). EP study may provide additional risk information but should not delay ICD placement. Even if EP study is negative, he remains high-risk.
why_wrong.c: Quinidine is used for recurrent ICD shocks or when ICD is contraindicated, not as first-line monotherapy in high-risk patients. It reduces arrhythmia burden but does NOT provide the same protection as an ICD.
why_wrong.d: This patient is NOT asymptomatic - he has syncope (likely arrhythmic). Spontaneous Type 1 pattern + syncope carries high risk, not benign. Reassurance without ICD could result in sudden death.
why_wrong.e: Catheter ablation for Brugada is experimental and reserved for patients with recurrent ICD shocks despite quinidine. It is NOT first-line and does not replace ICD therapy.

explanation.clinical_pearl: Brugada syndrome: autosomal dominant sodium channelopathy (SCN5A). Type 1 ECG: coved ST elevation ≥2mm in V1-V2 with T-wave inversion (diagnostic). High-risk features: spontaneous Type 1 + syncope + family history of sudden death. ICD is the ONLY proven therapy. Avoid: sodium channel blockers (procainamide, flecainide), hyperthermia, certain drugs (www.brugadadrugs.org). Quinidine for recurrent shocks. Screen all first-degree relatives.

question: A 55-year-old man with newly diagnosed hypertension (BP 155/95 confirmed on 2 separate visits) has no other medical conditions. He is a nonsmoker. Basic metabolic panel, lipid panel, and ECG are normal. He drinks 3-4 beers daily. Which is the MOST appropriate initial antihypertensive therapy?

options.text: Lisinopril 10mg daily, because ACE inhibitors are first-line for most patients with uncomplicated hypertension
Atenolol 50mg daily, because beta-blockers have been proven to reduce cardiovascular events in hypertension
Furosemide 20mg daily, because diuretics are the most effective monotherapy for hypertension
Clonidine 0.1mg BID, because centrally acting agents provide rapid blood pressure reduction
No medication; lifestyle modifications alone including reduced alcohol intake and sodium restriction for 6 months

explanation.why_correct: First-line antihypertensives per ACC/AHA 2017 guidelines include: ACEi/ARB, calcium channel blockers (CCB), or thiazide diuretics. Lisinopril (ACEi) is an appropriate first choice for this patient with uncomplicated Stage 2 hypertension. Lifestyle modifications (reduced alcohol, sodium restriction, exercise, weight loss) should be recommended concurrently but are insufficient alone for Stage 2 hypertension (BP ≥140/90).

why_wrong.b: Beta-blockers are no longer first-line for uncomplicated hypertension (JNC 8, ACC/AHA 2017). They are indicated if there is a compelling indication: CAD, HFrEF, post-MI. Atenolol specifically has NOT been shown to reduce cardiovascular events compared to other antihypertensives.
why_wrong.c: Thiazide diuretics (chlorthalidone, HCTZ) are first-line, not loop diuretics (furosemide). Furosemide is reserved for volume overload states (HF, CKD) and is less effective for chronic BP control due to short duration of action.
why_wrong.d: Clonidine is a third/fourth-line agent due to side effects (sedation, dry mouth, rebound hypertension if abruptly stopped). It is not appropriate for initial therapy in uncomplicated hypertension.
why_wrong.e: This patient has Stage 2 hypertension (SBP ≥140). Guidelines recommend pharmacologic therapy PLUS lifestyle modifications, not lifestyle alone. Delaying medication for 6 months leaves him at unnecessary cardiovascular risk.

explanation.clinical_pearl: ACC/AHA 2017 BP categories: Normal <120/<80, Elevated 120-129/<80, Stage 1 130-139/80-89, Stage 2 ≥140/≥90. First-line agents: ACEi/ARB, CCB, Thiazide. Compelling indications: HFrEF (ARNI/ACEi/ARB + BB + MRA), CKD (ACEi/ARB), CAD (BB + ACEi), DM (ACEi/ARB). Target BP <130/80 for most patients.

question: A 32-year-old intravenous drug user presents with fever (39.5°C), chills, and fatigue for 1 week. He has new-onset dyspnea and orthopnea. BP 100/60, HR 112, RR 24, SpO2 92%. Examination reveals splinter hemorrhages in nail beds, conjunctival petechiae, Janeway lesions on palms (nontender), and a new harsh holosystolic murmur grade 4/6 at the left lower sternal border. Blood cultures grow methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus in 4/4 bottles. What is the most appropriate treatment duration and route?

options.text: IV oxacillin or nafcillin 2g every 4 hours for 6 weeks; no need for gentamicin because this is native valve MSSA IE
IV vancomycin 15-20mg/kg every 12 hours for 2 weeks because right-sided IE in IVDU can be treated with shorter duration
IV oxacillin + gentamicin for 2 weeks (short-course combination therapy for uncomplicated MSSA IE)
Oral linezolid 600mg BID for 6 weeks after initial IV therapy, because oral regimens are equally effective
IV daptomycin 6mg/kg daily for 6 weeks because oxacillin cannot be used in IV drug users

explanation.why_correct: This patient has definite infective endocarditis (Duke criteria: 2 major = persistent bacteremia + new regurgitant murmur, plus multiple minor criteria). The organism is MSSA. For native valve MSSA IE, oxacillin/nafcillin 12g/day in 6 divided doses for 6 weeks is standard. Gentamicin is NOT recommended for native valve MSSA IE because it adds nephrotoxicity without proven benefit (AHA 2015 guidelines). The tricuspid valve is most commonly affected in IVDU, but 4/4 bottles positive with left-sided signs (pulmonary edema, orthopnea) suggests possible left-sided involvement requiring 6-week course.

why_wrong.b: 2-week course is ONLY for uncomplicated right-sided IE in IVDU (tricuspid valve, MSSA, no vegetations >2cm, no emboli, no heart failure). This patient has signs suggesting possible left-sided involvement (orthopnea, pulmonary edema), so 6 weeks is required.
why_wrong.c: Combination therapy with gentamicin for 2 weeks is NOT recommended for native valve IE (increased nephrotoxicity without mortality benefit). Gentamicin is reserved for prosthetic valve IE or enterococcal IE.
why_wrong.d: Oral antibiotics are NOT standard for IE. IV therapy is required to achieve sustained bactericidal levels. Linezolid is bacteriostatic against Staph, not first-line. Oral step-down is experimental and not recommended.
why_wrong.e: Oxacillin/nafcillin is the drug of choice for MSSA (superior to vancomycin). Daptomycin is an alternative for MRSA or penicillin allergy. IVDU is not a contraindication to oxacillin.

explanation.clinical_pearl: Duke criteria for IE: 2 major (persistent bacteremia + new murmur/vegetation) or 1 major + 3 minor. Native valve MSSA: Oxacillin/Nafcillin 12g/day ×6 weeks. Uncomplicated right-sided IE in IVDU: 2 weeks possible. Gentamicin NOT recommended for native valve Staph IE. Indications for surgery: Heart failure, perivalvular abscess, persistent bacteremia >5-7 days, fungal IE, prosthetic valve, recurrent emboli, vegetation >10mm.

question: A 65-year-old woman with hypertension and diabetes presents with acute chest pain for 30 minutes. ECG shows ST elevation of 2mm in II, III, aVF with reciprocal ST depression in I, aVL. BP 90/60, HR 48, RR 22. She is diaphoretic and nauseated. Which additional ECG leads should be obtained immediately?

options.text: Right-sided leads (V4R) to evaluate for right ventricular infarction, because inferior STEMI with hypotension and bradycardia suggests RV involvement
Posterior leads (V7-V9) to evaluate for posterior wall involvement
Repeat standard 12-lead ECG in 15 minutes to evaluate for dynamic changes
Lewis lead placement to better visualize atrial activity during bradycardia
No additional leads needed; the diagnosis of inferior STEMI is already established

explanation.why_correct: Inferior STEMI with hypotension and bradycardia is classic for right ventricular infarction. RV infarction occurs in 30-50% of inferior STEMIs (usually from proximal RCA occlusion). Diagnosis requires right-sided leads (V4R): ST elevation ≥1mm in V4R is diagnostic. This is critical because RV infarction requires: 1) Avoid nitroglycerin (decreases preload → severe hypotension), 2) Aggressive IV fluids (maintain RV preload), 3) Urgent reperfusion. Mortality is higher with RV involvement.

why_wrong.b: Posterior leads (V7-V9) evaluate for posterior wall infarction, which presents with ST depression in V1-V3 (mirror image of posterior STEMI). This patient has inferior STEMI, not posterior. Posterior leads would be indicated if V1-V3 showed ST depression.
why_wrong.c: Repeat ECG would delay diagnosis of RV infarction. Right-sided leads should be obtained immediately, not after waiting 15 minutes. The hypotension and bradycardia demand immediate action.
why_wrong.d: Lewis lead is used to better visualize P waves during arrhythmias (atrial flutter vs sinus tachycardia), not for STEMI evaluation. It has no role in acute MI management.
why_wrong.e: While inferior STEMI is diagnosed, the presence of RV infarction dramatically changes management (avoid nitrates, give fluids). Without right-sided leads, RV infarction cannot be confirmed or ruled out.

explanation.clinical_pearl: Inferior STEMI + hypotension + bradycardia = RV infarction until proven otherwise. Obtain V4R immediately. RV infarction management: AVOID nitroglycerin and other preload reducers, give IV fluids to maintain RV preload, urgent PCI. RV infarction increases mortality 2-3× compared to inferior STEMI without RV involvement. Bezold-Jarisch reflex: bradycardia + hypotension in inferior MI from vagal stimulation.

question: A 48-year-old woman with no cardiac history presents with palpitations triggered by stress and caffeine. Episodes last minutes and terminate abruptly. Baseline ECG shows sinus rhythm with short PR interval (100ms), wide QRS (130ms), and a slurred upstroke (delta wave) in leads I, aVL, V5-V6. During palpitations, ECG shows regular wide-complex tachycardia at 220 bpm. What is the mechanism of the tachycardia?

options.text: Antidromic AVRT: antegrade conduction via accessory pathway, retrograde via AV node, producing wide QRS tachycardia
Orthodromic AVRT: antegrade conduction via AV node, retrograde via accessory pathway, producing narrow QRS tachycardia
Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response via the accessory pathway
Ventricular tachycardia originating from the right ventricular outflow tract
AV nodal reentrant tachycardia (AVNRT) with aberrant conduction

explanation.why_correct: This patient has WPW syndrome (baseline: short PR, delta wave, wide QRS). During tachycardia, the QRS is WIDE (220 bpm, wide QRS). This is ANTIDROMIC AVRT: antegrade conduction via the accessory pathway (producing wide QRS) and retrograde via the AV node. Antidromic AVRT accounts for only 5% of WPW-related tachycardias (95% are orthodromic with narrow QRS). It can be difficult to distinguish from VT. Catheter ablation is curative.

why_wrong.b: Orthodromic AVRT produces NARROW QRS tachycardia (antegrade via AV node, retrograde via pathway). This patient has WIDE QRS tachycardia, consistent with antidromic AVRT.
why_wrong.c: AF with WPW shows irregularly irregular rhythm with varying QRS width (some beats conducted via AV node = narrow, some via pathway = wide). This tachycardia is REGULAR, ruling out AF.
why_wrong.d: VT is the most common wide-complex tachycardia, but this patient has known WPW (delta wave on baseline). The presence of WPW makes antidromic AVRT more likely than VT. Adenosine can help differentiate.
why_wrong.e: AVNRT is a narrow-complex tachycardia. Aberrant conduction can cause wide QRS, but the presence of WPW pattern on baseline ECG makes AVRT the most likely mechanism.

explanation.clinical_pearl: WPW: accessory pathway (Bundle of Kent) bypasses AV node → pre-excitation (delta wave, short PR, wide QRS). Orthodromic AVRT (95%): narrow QRS, antegrade AV node, retrograde pathway. Antidromic AVRT (5%): wide QRS, antegrade pathway, retrograde AV node (can mimic VT). AF with WPW: irregular, wide QRS, can degenerate to VF. AVOID AV nodal blockers (adenosine, verapamil, digoxin, beta-blockers) during AF with WPW (may cause 1:1 conduction and VF). Treatment: catheter ablation (>95% curative).

question: A 58-year-old man collapses while giving a presentation. Bystander CPR is initiated. EMS arrives 8 minutes later. Monitor shows ventricular fibrillation. Defibrillation with 200J is performed with return of spontaneous circulation after 2 shocks. He is intubated and comatose. BP 90/55 on norepinephrine. ECG shows ST elevation in V1-V4. What is the MOST important intervention to improve neurologic outcome?

options.text: Targeted temperature management (TTM) at 32-36°C for at least 24 hours, because hypothermia reduces cerebral metabolic demand and improves neurologic recovery after cardiac arrest
Emergent coronary angiography with PCI, because the STEMI is the underlying cause and immediate revascularization is required
Initiate IV amiodarone to prevent recurrent ventricular fibrillation and stabilize the cardiac rhythm
Obtain a stat head CT to rule out intracranial hemorrhage as the cause of cardiac arrest
Administer high-dose methylprednisolone to reduce cerebral edema and improve neurologic recovery

explanation.why_correct: After cardiac arrest with ROSC, targeted temperature management (TTM) at 32-36°C for ≥24 hours is the ONLY intervention proven to improve neurologic outcomes (TTM1 and TTM2 trials). It reduces cerebral metabolic rate, free radical production, apoptosis, and intracranial pressure. While emergent PCI is also critical (the STEMI needs revascularization), TTM should be initiated immediately and continued during and after PCI. Both interventions are essential, but TTM specifically targets neurologic recovery.

why_wrong.b: Emergent PCI is absolutely indicated for STEMI and should be performed, but PCI alone does not optimize neurologic recovery. TTM should be initiated concurrently and continued through and after PCI.
why_wrong.c: Amiodarone may be used for recurrent VF but does NOT improve neurologic outcome. The priority after ROSC is TTM and PCI, not empiric antiarrhythmics.
why_wrong.d: Cardiac arrest from STEMI is overwhelmingly more likely than ICH in this scenario (ST elevation present). Head CT should be considered if there is no clear cardiac cause, but delaying PCI and TTM for CT is harmful.
why_wrong.e: Steroids have NO proven benefit in post-cardiac arrest care and may worsen outcomes (increased infection, hyperglycemia). The 2020 AHA guidelines do not recommend routine steroid use.

explanation.clinical_pearl: Post-cardiac arrest care: 1) TTM at 32-36°C for ≥24h, 2) Emergent PCI if STEMI present, 3) Avoid hyperthermia (>37.7°C) for 72h, 4) Maintain MAP >65, SpO2 92-98%, normocapnia, 5) Neuroprognostication no earlier than 72h after rewarming. TTM improves survival with good neurologic outcome (NNT=6 for TTM at 33°C vs normothermia per TTM1 trial).

question: A 22-year-old college student presents with acute-onset sharp chest pain worse with deep inspiration and lying supine, improved by sitting forward. He had an upper respiratory infection 2 weeks ago. He is afebrile. Examination reveals a triphasic friction rub at the left sternal border. ECG shows diffuse ST elevation in all leads except aVR and V1, with PR depression in II and aVF. Troponin is normal. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Ibuprofen 600-800mg TID for 1-2 weeks plus colchicine 0.5mg BID for 3 months, with activity restriction until symptoms resolve
Prednisone 60mg daily with a slow taper over 4 weeks to reduce inflammation and prevent recurrence
Aspirin 975mg TID plus colchicine 0.5mg daily for 6 months, as aspirin is superior to NSAIDs for pericarditis
No pharmacologic therapy; reassurance and observation since viral pericarditis is self-limited
Pericardiocentesis to drain the effusion and relieve symptoms, because friction rub indicates a large effusion

explanation.why_correct: This is classic acute idiopathic/viral pericarditis (young, post-URI, positional chest pain, friction rub, diffuse ST elevation with PR depression, normal troponin). First-line treatment is NSAIDs (ibuprofen 600-800mg TID for 1-2 weeks, tapered based on symptoms) PLUS colchicine 0.5mg BID for 3 months. Colchicine reduces recurrence rate by 50% (COPE and ICAP trials). Activity restriction is recommended until symptoms resolve and CRP normalizes. This is Stage 1 ECG (diffuse ST elevation + PR depression).

why_wrong.b: Corticosteroids are NOT first-line for pericarditis. They increase recurrence rate 3-fold (COPPS trial). They should be reserved for refractory/recurrent cases, autoimmune etiology, uremic pericarditis, or pregnancy (contraindication to NSAIDs).
why_wrong.c: Aspirin is preferred POST-MI (Dressler syndrome) because NSAIDs impair myocardial healing. For idiopathic pericarditis, NSAIDs are first-line. Colchicine is dosed 0.5mg BID, not daily.
why_wrong.d: While viral pericarditis is self-limited, treatment reduces symptoms, duration, and most importantly RECURRENCE. Untreated pericarditis has 30% recurrence rate vs 10% with colchicine.
why_wrong.e: Pericardiocentesis is indicated for cardiac tamponade (Beck triad: hypotension, muffled heart sounds, JVD) or large effusions causing hemodynamic compromise. This patient has no evidence of tamponade or significant effusion.

explanation.clinical_pearl: Acute pericarditis ECG stages: Stage 1-diffuse ST elevation+PR depression, Stage 2-pseudonormalization, Stage 3-diffuse T wave inversion, Stage 4-normalization. NSAIDs first-line (ibuprofen 600-800mg TID). Colchicine 0.5mg BID ×3 months reduces recurrence 50%. Avoid steroids first-line. CRP monitoring to guide NSAID taper.

question: A 78-year-old woman with severe aortic stenosis (valve area 0.6 cm², mean gradient 55 mmHg), hypertension, and COPD presents with progressive dyspnea and chest pressure on minimal exertion. She is deemed high risk for surgical AVR (STS score 9.5%). She has a trileaflet aortic valve with severe calcification. Which intervention has the best evidence for improving survival in this patient?

options.text: Transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR) via transfemoral approach, because TAVR is superior to medical therapy in inoperable patients and non-inferior/superior to SAVR in high-risk patients
Surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR) with bioprosthetic valve, because surgery remains the gold standard for all patients with aortic stenosis
Balloon aortic valvuloplasty (BAV) as a bridge to definitive therapy or as destination therapy in high-risk patients
Medical therapy with afterload reduction (ACE inhibitors) and diuretics, because the surgical risk outweighs the benefit
Ross procedure (pulmonary autograft) because it provides the best long-term durability in younger patients

explanation.why_correct: This patient has severe symptomatic AS with high surgical risk (STS 9.5%). The PARTNER 1A trial demonstrated TAVR is non-inferior to SAVR for mortality in high-risk patients. PARTNER 2 and SURTAVI trials extended this to intermediate-risk patients. PARTNER 3 and EVOLUT Low Risk trials showed TAVR is superior or non-inferior to SAVR even in low-risk patients. For this elderly patient with comorbidities, TAVR via transfemoral approach is the standard of care. The trileaflet valve anatomy is favorable for TAVR.

why_wrong.b: While SAVR is the gold standard for low-risk younger patients, this elderly patient with STS 9.5% is high-risk. TAVR has equivalent or superior outcomes in this population with shorter recovery, less pain, and lower perioperative morbidity.
why_wrong.c: BAV provides only temporary relief (valve area increases 0.3-0.4 cm²) with restenosis within 6-12 months. It is used as a bridge to definitive therapy in unstable patients or as palliation, not as destination therapy.
why_wrong.d: Medical therapy for severe symptomatic AS does NOT improve survival. Once symptoms develop, mortality is 25-50% at 1 year without valve replacement. This patient needs intervention, not medical management.
why_wrong.e: The Ross procedure is for young patients (typically <50 years) who need long-term durability and wish to avoid anticoagulation. It is completely inappropriate for a 78-year-old with comorbidities.

explanation.clinical_pearl: TAVR indications (2020 ACC/AHA): Recommended for inoperable patients (Class I), high-risk (Class I), intermediate-risk (Class IIa), and low-risk (Class IIa) patients. TAVR is preferred over SAVR in patients ≥80 years or younger patients with life expectancy <10 years. SAVR is preferred in patients <65 years, bicuspid AV, need for concomitant CABG, or unfavorable TAVR anatomy.

question: A 68-year-old diabetic woman presents with acute chest pain for 3 hours. ECG shows ST depression of 2mm in V4-V6 with T wave inversion. Troponin I is 4.5 ng/mL (elevated). BP 140/85, HR 95, SpO2 96%. She has ongoing chest discomfort. What is the most appropriate management strategy?

options.text: Start aspirin, ticagrelor, heparin, and plan for early invasive strategy (coronary angiography within 24 hours), because this is NSTEMI with high-risk features (elevated troponin, ST depression, diabetes)
Start aspirin and heparin, admit to telemetry, and perform stress testing prior to discharge to determine if angiography is needed
Activate the cardiac catheterization laboratory for emergent PCI within 90 minutes, because NSTEMI should be treated the same as STEMI
Start aspirin and clopidogrel, discharge home with cardiology follow-up in 1 week for outpatient stress testing
Administer thrombolytics immediately because the patient is within the 12-hour window for fibrinolytic therapy

explanation.why_correct: This patient has NSTEMI (ST depression + elevated troponin). Per AHA/ACC guidelines, an early invasive strategy (angiography within 24 hours) is indicated for high-risk NSTEMI patients. High-risk features include: elevated troponin, ST segment deviation, GRACE score >140, diabetes, age >65, and ongoing chest pain. She has multiple high-risk features (troponin elevation, ST depression, diabetes, age). DAPT (aspirin + ticagrelor) and anticoagulation (heparin) should be started immediately.

why_wrong.b: Stress testing is for low-risk NSTEMI patients without ongoing symptoms. This patient is high-risk (elevated troponin, ST depression, diabetes, ongoing pain). She needs early invasive strategy, not conservative management with stress testing.
why_wrong.c: NSTEMI does NOT require emergent PCI within 90 minutes (unlike STEMI). The timing depends on risk stratification: immediate (<2h) for refractory angina/hemodynamic instability, early (<24h) for high-risk, and delayed (25-72h) for intermediate-risk.
why_wrong.d: Discharging a high-risk NSTEMI patient home without angiography is dangerous. She has ongoing chest pain and elevated troponin. She requires inpatient management with early angiography.
why_wrong.e: Thrombolytics are CONTRAINDICATED in NSTEMI (TIMI IIIB trial showed harm). Thrombolytics are only for STEMI when PCI is unavailable. NSTEMI is managed with antiplatelets + anticoagulation + early invasive strategy.

explanation.clinical_pearl: NSTEMI management: DAPT (aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor) + anticoagulation (heparin/enoxaparin). Risk stratify with GRACE score. Early invasive (<24h) for high-risk: elevated troponin, ST changes, GRACE >140, diabetes, age >65, ongoing pain. Immediate invasive (<2h) for: refractory angina, hemodynamic instability, arrhythmias. NEVER give thrombolytics for NSTEMI.

question: A 35-year-old pregnant woman (32 weeks gestation) presents with acute dyspnea, orthopnea, and bilateral leg edema developing over 5 days. She has no prior cardiac history. BP 150/95, HR 110, RR 26, SpO2 91%. Echo shows severely reduced LV function (EF 25%), global hypokinesis, and moderate mitral regurgitation. Coronary angiography is normal. She delivered a healthy baby 3 months ago with a similar but milder episode that resolved spontaneously. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Peripartum cardiomyopathy, because new-onset heart failure with reduced EF occurring in the last month of pregnancy or first 5 months postpartum without another identifiable cause
Dilated cardiomyopathy from viral myocarditis, because acute viral infection is the most common cause of new-onset cardiomyopathy in young adults
Preeclampsia-induced heart failure, because hypertension in pregnancy can cause acute pulmonary edema
Amniotic fluid embolism, because acute dyspnea and cardiovascular collapse in a pregnant woman is classic for this diagnosis
Stress-induced cardiomyopathy (Takotsubo), because emotional stress of pregnancy can cause transient LV dysfunction

explanation.why_correct: Peripartum cardiomyopathy (PPCM) is defined as new-onset HFrEF (EF <45%) occurring in the last month of pregnancy or within 5 months postpartum, without another identifiable cause. This patient fits all criteria: 32 weeks (last month of pregnancy), EF 25%, normal coronaries (rules out ischemic), and prior episode 3 months postpartum that resolved (PPCM can recur in subsequent pregnancies with 30-50% risk). Incidence is 1:1000-4000 deliveries, higher in African descent, older age, and multiple gestations.

why_wrong.b: Viral myocarditis is common in young adults but the temporal relationship to pregnancy (two episodes - both peripartum) makes PPCM far more likely. Myocarditis would show late gadolinium enhancement on cardiac MRI (focal), while PPCM is typically global.
why_wrong.c: Preeclampsia causes diastolic dysfunction with preserved EF (HFpEF pattern), not severely reduced EF (25%). The hypertension is secondary to HF in this case, not primary preeclampsia.
why_wrong.d: Amniotic fluid embolism presents with SUDDEN cardiovascular collapse, respiratory failure, and DIC during labor or immediately postpartum. This patient has a subacute course over 5 days and is stable enough for echo.
why_wrong.e: Takotsubo typically occurs in postmenopausal women after emotional stress, shows apical ballooning on echo, and resolves within weeks. Pregnancy is not a typical trigger, and recurrence is rare unlike PPCM.

explanation.clinical_pearl: PPCM: EF <45% in last month of pregnancy to 5 months postpartum without other cause. Risk factors: African descent, age >30, multiparity, multiple gestation, preeclampsia, tocolytic therapy. Treatment: Standard HFrEF therapy (ACEi/ARB contraindicated in pregnancy - use hydralazine+nitrates; safe postpartum). Bromocriptine may improve recovery (prolactin fragment implicated). Recovery: 50-60% recover EF within 6 months. Recurrence risk in subsequent pregnancy: 30-50%. Mortality 5-10%.

question: A 60-year-old man with hypertension and smoking history presents with sudden-onset severe interscapular pain radiating to the abdomen. BP 210/120, HR 100. CXR shows widened mediastinum with obliteration of the aortic knob. ECG shows sinus tachycardia with no ST changes. What is the most appropriate sequence of management?

options.text: Start IV esmolol to reduce HR to <60 and BP to 100-120 systolic, then obtain CT angiography of the chest/abdomen to confirm the diagnosis
Start IV nitroprusside to rapidly reduce blood pressure to normal, then obtain transesophageal echocardiography
Obtain CT angiography immediately before starting any medications, because antihypertensives may interfere with the imaging
Activate the cardiac catheterization laboratory for emergent angiography to rule out acute coronary syndrome
Start aspirin and heparin immediately for suspected acute coronary syndrome, then obtain CT to rule out dissection

explanation.why_correct: This patient has classic acute aortic syndrome (likely Type B aortic dissection): sudden severe interscapular pain, malignant hypertension, widened mediastinum on CXR. The first step is immediate blood pressure control to reduce aortic wall stress. Beta-blockers (esmolol) must be given FIRST to reduce HR (<60) and dP/dt before vasodilators. This prevents reflex tachycardia from vasodilators which would increase aortic wall stress. Target SBP 100-120. Then obtain definitive imaging (CT angiography) to confirm and classify the dissection.

why_wrong.b: Nitroprusside without beta-blockade causes reflex tachycardia and increased dP/dt, which can EXTEND the dissection. Beta-blockers MUST precede vasodilators. TEE is excellent but invasive; CT angiography is more readily available and less invasive.
why_wrong.c: Imaging should be obtained promptly but NOT before blood pressure control. BP must be reduced immediately to prevent extension, rupture, or malperfusion. The patient can be stabilized while CT is being arranged.
why_wrong.d: This presentation is not ACS (no ST changes, interscapular pain, widened mediastinum). Activating cath lab would delay correct diagnosis and potentially harm the patient if antiplatelets are given. ACS protocol is contraindicated in dissection.
why_wrong.e: Aspirin and heparin are ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in aortic dissection. Antiplatelets and anticoagulants worsen bleeding and increase mortality. This is a critical error that can be fatal.

explanation.clinical_pearl: Aortic dissection: Type A (ascending) = surgical emergency, Type B (descending) = medical management unless complicated. Initial management: Beta-blocker FIRST (esmolol to HR <60), then vasodilators to SBP 100-120. Target HR <60 and lowest BP that maintains organ perfusion. NEVER give antiplatelets or anticoagulants. CT angiography is diagnostic gold standard. TEE if CT unavailable or renal failure.

question: A 62-year-old man with HFrEF (EF 35%) on lisinopril 20mg, carvedilol 25mg BID, and spironolactone 25mg presents for follow-up. BP 110/70, HR 64, eGFR 50, K+ 4.5. He remains NYHA Class II with mild dyspnea on exertion. Which medication addition provides BOTH mortality reduction and heart failure hospitalization reduction in this patient regardless of diabetes status?

options.text: Dapagliflozin 10mg daily (SGLT2 inhibitor), because DAPA-HF and EMPEROR-Reduced trials showed mortality benefit in HFrEF independent of diabetes status
Sacubitril-valsartan (replacing lisinopril), because ARNI is superior to ACEi for reducing mortality and HF hospitalizations in HFrEF
Ivabradine 5mg BID, because it reduces cardiovascular mortality and HF hospitalizations when added to optimal GDMT
Digoxin 0.125mg daily, because it reduces HF hospitalizations and has been a standard therapy for decades
Vericiguat 10mg daily, because it reduces cardiovascular death and HF hospitalizations in high-risk HFrEF patients

explanation.why_correct: SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) reduce cardiovascular death and HF hospitalizations in HFrEF patients regardless of diabetes status. DAPA-HF trial: dapagliflozin reduced CV death or worsening HF by 26% (NNT=21). EMPEROR-Reduced: empagliflozin reduced CV death or HF hospitalization by 25%. These benefits were seen in patients already on optimal GDMT. SGLT2i are now one of the 4 pillars of GDMT for HFrEF (ARNI/ACEi/ARB + BB + MRA + SGLT2i).

why_wrong.b: Switching to ARNI is also beneficial (PARADIGM-HF: 20% reduction in CV death/HF hospitalization vs enalapril) and should be considered. However, the question asks about ADDING a medication, not replacing one. Both ARNI switch and SGLT2i addition are recommended.
why_wrong.c: Ivabradine reduces CV death and HF hospitalization when HR >70 despite beta-blockers (SHIFT trial). This patient's HR is 64, so ivabradine is not indicated.
why_wrong.d: Digoxin reduces HF hospitalizations but does NOT reduce mortality (DIG trial). It is used for symptom control, not as disease-modifying therapy.
why_wrong.e: Vericiguat (VICTORIA trial) reduces CV death and HF hospitalization in high-risk HFrEF patients with recent decompensation. This stable outpatient does not meet criteria for vericiguat.

explanation.clinical_pearl: HFrEF GDMT 4 pillars: ARNI/ACEi/ARB + Beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i. DAPA-HF: dapagliflozin 26% reduction in CV death/worsening HF. EMPEROR-Reduced: empagliflozin 25% reduction. Benefits are INDEPENDENT of diabetes status. SGLT2i should be added to existing GDMT, not replace it. Monitor for euglycemic DKA, volume depletion, and genital infections.

question: A 40-year-old man presents with recurrent episodes of hypertension, palpitations, headache, and diaphoresis that last 10-15 minutes and resolve spontaneously. During an episode, BP is 220/130, HR 130. Between episodes, BP is 130/80. 24-hour urine metanephrines are elevated 5 times the upper limit of normal. CT abdomen shows a 4cm right adrenal mass with Hounsfield units >25 on non-contrast imaging. What is the most appropriate preoperative management?

options.text: Start phenoxybenzamine (alpha-blocker) for 10-14 days before surgery, followed by beta-blocker only after adequate alpha-blockade, along with high-sodium diet and IV fluids to expand intravascular volume
Start labetalol (combined alpha/beta blocker) for 7 days before surgery, because it simplifies the preoperative regimen
Proceed directly to laparoscopic adrenalectomy without preoperative medical management, because the tumor needs urgent removal
Start amlodipine for blood pressure control and schedule elective adrenalectomy in 4-6 weeks
Start metoprolol first to control tachycardia, then add amlodipine for blood pressure control before surgery

explanation.why_correct: This patient has pheochromocytoma (paroxysmal hypertension + classic triad + elevated metanephrines + adrenal mass with HU >25). Preoperative alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine 10mg BID, titrated up) for 10-14 days is mandatory before surgery to prevent intraoperative hypertensive crisis. Beta-blockers are added ONLY AFTER adequate alpha-blockade (usually 3-4 days later) to control reflex tachycardia. High-sodium diet and IV fluids expand the catecholamine-depleted intravascular volume to prevent severe postoperative hypotension. Surgery without proper preparation carries mortality up to 50%.

why_wrong.b: Labetalol has weak alpha-blockade relative to beta-blockade (1:7 ratio). It is NOT sufficient as sole agent for pheochromocytoma. Phenoxybenzamine (irreversible alpha-blocker) is the gold standard for preoperative preparation.
why_wrong.c: Operating on pheochromocytoma without preoperative alpha-blockade is EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. Manipulation of the tumor releases massive catecholamines → severe hypertension, arrhythmias, myocardial infarction, and death.
why_wrong.d: Calcium channel blockers alone are inadequate for pheochromocytoma. While they may be used as adjuncts, alpha-blockade is the cornerstone of preoperative management. Surgery should not be delayed unnecessarily once the diagnosis is confirmed.
why_wrong.e: Starting beta-blocker BEFORE alpha-blocker causes unopposed alpha stimulation → vasoconstriction → hypertensive crisis. This is a classic board examination point. Alpha-blockade must precede beta-blockade in pheochromocytoma.

explanation.clinical_pearl: Pheochromocytoma: Rule of 10s - 10% bilateral, 10% extra-adrenal, 10% malignant, 10% familial, 10% children. Diagnosis: plasma free metanephrines (sensitivity 97%) or 24h urinary metanephrines. Preoperative: Alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine) for 10-14 days → add beta-blocker 3-4 days later → high-sodium diet + IV fluids day before surgery. NEVER start beta-blocker before alpha-blocker. Genetic testing if <50 years (RET, VHL, NF1, SDHx).
